sentences.text: Good afternoon, Paula.
My name is Rena Finder.
F-I-N-D-E-R.
My first name is Rena, R-E-N-A.
My maiden name is Ferber, F-E-R-B-E-R.
February 24, 1929.
I was born in Cracow, Poland.
It was [NON-ENGLISH], which means [INAUDIBLE] Street, number 8, apartment 12.
Well, it was a brand new apartment when I was born.
We moved in there just when I was a few months old.
It was right across the street from the river and right next to a castle.
And I always thought it was the most beautiful building ever.
It was-- it was a big building.
It had four flights.
And on each floor there were about four apartments.
And from the back yard, I could see the old church that belonged to the Franciscan brothers.
It was beautiful.
We had a beautiful apartment.
It was probably not beautiful for today's standards, but those days it was new.
It had hot water in a bath-- bathroom.
And-- it was very-- I said it was beautiful.
I still do.
My-- my mother and father.
I'm an only child.
My mother was Roa, or we would call her Roza.
And my father was Moniek, and his given name was Moses.
My mother's maiden name was Windisch.
W-I-N-D-I-S-C-H.
My father was a salesman for a company that was making surgical instruments and surgical supplies.
And he-- he was traveling a lot.
He would leave every morning.
Every Monday morning he would leave.
He traveled by train.
And he would come home most of the weeks on Friday night, just in time for Shabbat.
Yeah.
Since I was an only child, I spent a lot of time with my mother and her sister, because she-- her sister, unmarried younger sister, lived around the corner from our house with my grandparents.
My mother's parents were Hannah-- Hannah Shifra, I remember.
And my grandfather was Isaac.
My-- well, we called her Binka, but her name was Berta.
You know, Cracow's not such a big city, so my father's family lived very close by, a few blocks away in the old part of Cracow, the Kazimierz.
And my mother's two brothers, who they were married-- one of them had a little girl-- they lived across the river in a place called Podgorze.
My other uncle, my mother's brother and sister, lived in Berlin.
All the time.
Well, of course, the family that lived in Cracow, we got together often.
But the family who was in Berlin would come every summer, and we would go to the country together.
We went to a place called Zawoya, which is in the mountains.
And we would rent-- my parents would rent a cottage that belonged to the peasants.
And they would rent it out and moved into the barn for the summer.
They were making money that way.
And we had a wonderful vacation.
Usually there were-- my parents used to go hiking in the woods.
And we used to go swimming in the river, little streams and used to go pick strawberries and blueberries in the forest, and mushrooms.
I don't remember him taking the whole summer off, but I do remember him coming and probably staying for a week or so and then, you know, leaving again.
Everybody I knew, my family and friends, were Conservative, more on the Orthodox side.
Because all the synagogues, all the temples, all the shuls that I knew, men and women were separated.
And there was just one Reform synagogue in Cracow, but I really didn't know anybody that belonged.
I guess we must have.
I don't really know.
But my-- I always went to visit my mother and my grandparents in the shul for High Holy Days.
My mother's parents did not belong to the same temple as my father's parents.
But they were, you know, like almost across the street, so I would go and make my rounds, go from one temple to another, especially on Yom Kippur.
I used to make apples.
I used to put cloves in the apples and bring it to my grandmother's for Yom Kippur, because, of course, they fasted.
They used to smell the apples.
The smell was supposed to revive them.
I think probably Passover.
I loved Passover because at Passover we went to my-- our maternal grandparents.
And for a long time, I was the only child there because my youngest cousin wasn't born yet and the other cousins who lived in Berlin did not come for Passover.
So I was the only one, and I was to sit next to my grandfather.
Passover, the Seder was conducted in the bedroom.
The table was brought to the back so my grandfather could sit on all the down pillows and recline, just-- just like you're supposed to do.
And I would sit next to him.
And I was probably two years old when I started to ask the four questions.
And I remember, you know, how much-- how proud I was, because they made such a big deal about me being able to do that.
When you're an only child and an only grandchild, I don't know, it's very special.
Well, I remember a lot of helping, you know, when my mother was making-- when she was baking, I used to lick the spoon.
And I remember licking the pots and pans from the ducks or geese when they were-- but I really didn't cook.
I was too young.
Didn't do much.
Yeah.
The Seder is-- I remember the Seders vividly.

And I do remember, as I got a little older, that I would-- you know, at Yom Kippur, in the Erev Yom Kippur, I had to hurry up and finish dinner early so my parents could go to the temple, to the synagogue.
And then going with the nanny the day of Yom Kippur, bringing the apples to my mother and my grandmothers and visiting.
And I think I stayed very long in the temple, in the shul, when I was young.
Because my parents, my grandparents went for the whole day.
It was a Polish girl that came from the country, probably from where we used to go for the summer.
And they like to do-- they love to come to the city.
And, you know, they had room and board.
Yeah.
Yeah.
In the kitchen, they had like a small room, and that's where she slept.
No, I don't.
I don't.
Yes, I went to school right around the corner from my house.
I just walked not-- probably not even a block.
And the school was so close that I could see my house from-- from the classroom.
And always when I played at recess, I could see my house.
And my mother, a lot of time she would stand on the balcony, you know, in the back of the house, and she could see me play when the weather was nice.
So I really always thought I had a wonderful childhood.
It was called [POLISH] 12.
And it was an all girls-- all girls school, because I think all the public schools were segregated also.
Boys and girls did not attend school together.
No.
It was a public school.

My-- I did-- I wasn't-- my mother didn't want to let me go to the Jewish school, the private school, because she couldn't see me walking alone, the distance, which really wasn't that far, and a lot of the children walked.
So she always said, when you go to sixth grade, when you go to sixth grade.
Of course I never went to sixth grade.
Not really, not really.
I-- I remember the Jewish girls I played with went to school-- dancing school, gymnastic, and Hebrew school.
Polish girls, there were very few.
And I think we played at recess.
But once I remember I was invited to a Polish girl's house, and once she came to my house.
No, we never heard any-- any contact.
I think my parents had only one Polish family they were friendly with, and this was a woman who was the superintendent of the building we lived in.
And she had three sons, and she had a husband who drank a lot and abused her, and my father was always intervening for her.
And finally, she divorced him, and then my father and my mother were helping her a lot and got the jobs for the boys.
The boys were older, and they were-- they were wonderful people.
The boys were wonderful, and the mother was a wonderful, wonderful lady.
We used to go there for Christmas Eve.
We would go there, that I remember, for dinner.
Well, it was a mixed-- mixed neighborhood.
But I would say probably 75% of the people in that building and around me were Jewish.
I remember anti-Semitism as long as I can remember, from what my parents would tell me, from what my grandparents would tell me.
They were telling me about pogroms.
And then I always remembered the feeling he would get from-- from the Polish people, always some kind of a remark and always feeling like you don't belong.

My parents were-- my grandparents were telling me of the pogroms, which happened, you know, years ago, years before in-- in Poland, in Russia, how people had to-- were murdered, people had to emigrate, and how being a-- being Jewish in Poland, we never felt that we belonged, even though they lived there for, you know, for hundreds of years.
But we never-- the Polish people never made us feel like we belonged, even though the Jewish people were very patriotic, like during the First War.
My uncles, my grandfathers, they were all in the army.
And my oldest uncle, my mother's oldest brother, was a surgeon during the first war.
And he was killed on a battlefield.
And he was awarded one of the highest medals that Poland had to offer.
And my grandparents were very proud of it.
His name was Zigmund.
And all I-- I have picture of-- I had pictures of him.
And I used to take the medal to school for show and tell.
You know, it was-- he-- he was killed.
They were very proud of him, because I guess he was so smart, and he was able to go to school and become a doctor.
And then he was killed.
Well, I remember in 1937-- you mean the Second War, by the way?
Yeah. 1937, I remember hearing talk about Hitler.
And in 1937, when my aunt and uncle from Berlin came, they were talking when they thought the children were asleep, when my cousin and I were asleep.
And they were saying how terrible things were, how-- for the Jews in Germany and Berlin.
And they were afraid.
But they've lived that.
For so many years, they had businesses.
They owned businesses.
My aunt and her husband was-- they were tailor and dressmaker.
And my uncle also had another business.
My mother's brother was also a tailor.
So they lived in Germany for a long time.
They considered themselves, you know, Germans.
So they did not have a citizenship.
Because if you didn't live in Germany, I think you couldn't get a citizenship.
But that's when I started hearing about Hitler.
I don't understand.
Your aunt and uncle lived in Germany?
My aunt and uncle immigrated to Berlin from Cracow and were able to get jobs over there.
Why they went, I don't know.
I never asked.
I was, you know, too young to ask.
And when I got old enough, there was nobody to ask.
No.
They didn't have citizenship because they were born in Poland, and they were Jews.
So I remember them talking about it.
But, you know, it was something was happening over there, not-- not to me.
My life still was the same.
They went back, yes.
They went back until 1939, when my aunt and uncle, my mother's sister, came in the middle of the night with her two little-- with the two little girls.
And they were told that they had to leave everything behind.
They just came with nothing.
So they lived was us, and they lived-- they sort of shared their time between our house, our apartment, and my grandmother's, who lived right around the corner.
But the uncle, my mother's brother, with his wife and son, they never came to Poland.
And I have never been able to find out what happened to them, you know?
Of course, I know they were murdered, but I don't know how and where.
Did they go to Auschwitz?
Did they go to Brunnlizt?
I don't know.
So when did life start to change for you?
Well, life started to change when the war broke out in-- actually, we were away in this country.
And my father came unexpectedly.
And he told us we had to leave.
So instead of leaving probably on the day that we would have left, we left earlier.
And I remember we took a train, and the train was crowded.
And I remember my parents very upset.
They were very upset.
And then when we got to Cracow, we probably were home maybe for a week when the war broke out.
Then, of course, it was a very tense time.
Because I remember them talking about it and being very scared.
And the question was, will the war start, and will the war not start?
And of course, by that time, already Czechoslovakia was under Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the war started.
Well, there were-- there were a lot of air raids.
And we used to have to go downstairs to the cellar.
And we could hear just after a few days-- Cracow was never bombed.
So the planes were probably going to Warsaw.
But as time was-- as days passed, you could hear the war coming closer and closer.
You could hear the cannons.
And we still stayed in the-- in the cellar every night.
We were very scared.
No.
Everybody was in their own house.
Oh, yeah, my aunt and uncle from Berlin--
Yeah.
They were with us.
They were with us.
That was very scary.
And then-- then the Germans invaded the whole Poland, and they came into Cracow.
The war started on September 1, and the Germans walked into Cracow on September 7.
Oh, I remember the day, because my father and his brothers-- my father was one of eight brothers.
And a lot of the neighbors who were Jewish, the Jewish men, were afraid.
And they decided they will try to escape.
They will go towards Lvov.
Because at that point, they felt Germans would not attack because they were supposed to sign an agreement, peace agreement with Russia.
So they left us.
All the man left.
From the whole building, I think, all the men left.
And all of my uncles left, thinking the war would be over very soon and then they will come back.
Yes, they thought that women and children would be safe.
They were afraid for the men.
But they felt, you know, the Germans are not going to do anything to the women and the children.
They went to Lemberg.
It was Lemberg.
And I felt t-- I was very afraid when my father left.
And then right after, the Germans came in.
And you could-- I could hear the trucks, I could hear the tanks, and I could hear the marching.
It sounded, you know, like a hurricane.
There were very-- a lot of noise.
And since I lived so close to the Wawel-- that's where they came.
The Wawel, the castle, the old castle, that's what they had-- was a headquarter for this part of Poland.
And that's where Frank--
But I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't remember exactly how long it was before my father came back.
I think probably it was just a couple of months.
Because when of course then Germany declared war on Russia and I guess all the men felt that it would be safe for them to come back.
Or they had to come back because of their families.
And my father and his brothers who were all married, they came back.
And the single brothers remain.
He had two single brothers.
They remained in Lemberg.
But my father and my uncles and the neighbors, they had to smuggle themselves back to Poland at that time.
And they had to swim the Bug River.
And not everyone was a good swimmer.
And my father was a very good swimmer, and my father saved a lot of people.
So when they started to come back, the men started to come back, I remember I was standing outside of the house one afternoon.
And I saw one of my neighbors come back.
One of my boyfriend's fathers came back.
And I just knew in my heart that my father would come back, too.
And my father came back the next day.
But the period of time before my father came back-- and again, I-- I don't remember.
I remember there was a Jewish holiday.
And of course, as soon as the Germans came in, we couldn't go to the temple anymore.
We couldn't go to the synagogues anymore.
We couldn't go to schools anymore.
So the men in the neighborhood would come to pray in our apartment.
And then we would be downstairs.
The children would be downstairs watching, making sure that the Germans were not coming.

So it was like two kids were out on a street, and two kids would be like on a second floor so that if we saw the Germans coming, we could call up and the other kids would run into the apartment and tell the people.
But we were lucky, and they didn't come.
They never came?
They never came while we prayed.
When they came, they were-- when they came they were looking to take gold and silver and brass and furs.
I had a little dog, and we were not allowed to have dogs.
One night-- this was even before we had to wear the armbands.
I was outside one evening with the little dog.
And the German patrol came by and said, what are you doing out on a street?
They didn't know at the time I was Jewish.
I didn't have to wear a star.
And I told them I had to take the dog out.
And they didn't, you know, do anything to me, but it scared my parents enough to give the dog away to the Polish woman that used to bring milk to the house in the beginning.
After a few months, the Polish people were not allowed to do any business with the Jews, and they couldn't bring any milk.
I think the only milk you could buy was from the peasants who used to come and sell to the houses.
So we had a woman that used to come and bring milk every day.
And we had no refrigerators, no iceboxes.
We started to wear the armband probably at the end of 1939.
And in Cracow, we wore a white armband with a blue Star of David.
Scared-- very scared.
Very scared because we were marked, and there were so many things we were not allowed to do.
We were not allowed to use the public transportation.
We were not allowed to walk on a sidewalk.
When-- when you saw a German soldier walk, you had to step off, get off the sidewalk.
And it was at that time, though, that we still-- of course we were not allowed to go to school.
But it was at that time we still were able to have lessons, private lessons.
And again, probably four of my girlfriends would have school in different houses every morning.
And of course, it was in secret.
So you had to be careful when you walked the street so nobody would see you.
You couldn't carry any books.
You couldn't carry any paper.
You were not supposed to learn.
But we would meet in different homes.
And we had professors that would come and teach us.
Because I was supposed to go into fifth grade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I never did.
When you were at these learning places, did you have to have guards then, too?
Did you feel-- Yeah.
They were the parents.
Mothers or somebody was always watching to make sure that the guards-- patrols were not coming.
And there was nobody-- if somebody would have spied on us, they would, you know, report us to the Gestapo.
But we were so young, and there was-- our parents wanted to make sure that we were learning.
Because at that time, they were still expecting the war not to last very long.
They still expected England will get involved and France will get involved, and-- and surely it can't go on very long.
You know, how can all this be happening and nobody will-- can-- is doing anything about it?
It just won't last.
OK, we're going to stop here, and we'll change tapes.
OK.
